title: Time-Series Database (InfluxDB)
description: InfluxDB is a purpose-built open-source time-series database with high write throughput. Offers InfluxQL and Flux query languages, native downsampling, and retention policies. Best for: IoT telemetry, real-time analytics, monitoring systems, self-hosted deployments. Optimized for time-stamped data with tags and fields.

provides.reasoning: InfluxDB is a purpose-built time-series database system that stores, indexes, and queries time-stamped data with tags and fields, offering complete database capabilities beyond just storage.
InfluxDB natively supports Flux as a functional data scripting language for querying, analyzing, and transforming time-series data, mentioned explicitly in the pattern description.
InfluxDB provides InfluxQL as an SQL-like query language for time-series data, explicitly mentioned alongside Flux in the pattern description as a core query capability.
InfluxDB offers native retention policies for automatic time-based data lifecycle management, explicitly highlighted in the pattern description as a key feature.
Native downsampling capability is explicitly mentioned in the pattern description, allowing automatic reduction of data resolution over time for efficient storage and querying.
InfluxDB supports continuous queries for automatic downsampling and pre-aggregation of time-series data, enabling real-time data transformation and summarization.
The pattern description explicitly highlights high write throughput as a key characteristic, optimized for ingesting large volumes of time-stamped data from IoT telemetry and monitoring systems.

requires.reasoning: InfluxDB requires underlying storage infrastructure to durably persist time-series data across database operations and restarts.
Database workloads require CPU and memory resources for data ingestion, query processing, compaction, and downsampling operations.

deployment_type.description: Self-hosted for full control and cost optimization, or InfluxDB Cloud for managed service with automatic scaling

retention_days.description: Data retention policy duration in days. Shorter retention reduces storage costs, longer retention enables historical analysis

shard_duration_hours.description: Shard group duration in hours (typically 1 week = 168). Shorter shards improve deletion performance, longer shards reduce metadata overhead

compression_enabled.description: Enable TSI (Time Series Index) compression to reduce disk usage at slight CPU cost for reads/writes

query_language.description: Flux is modern functional language with advanced transformations; InfluxQL is SQL-like legacy language, simpler but less powerful

replication_factor.description: Number of data copies across cluster nodes. Higher values increase availability and read performance but consume more storage

write_consistency.description: Write acknowledgment level: 'one' for speed, 'quorum' for balance, 'all' for maximum durability

service_auth_method.description: InfluxDB authentication method. 'token' (API token) is recommended; 'username_password' is legacy InfluxDB 1.x style.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min $0 for self-hosted InfluxDB OSS on existing infrastructure or InfluxDB Cloud free tier (up to 30 days retention, limited write/query limits). Max $500 represents mid-sized production deployments: self-hosted on cloud VMs ($100-200 for r5.large/similar with storage) or InfluxDB Cloud usage-based pricing for moderate IoT/monitoring workloads (10-50GB data retention, 100K-500K writes/sec). Enterprise production scenarios with high cardinality data and large clusters can exceed $2000+/month but $500 captures typical SMB monitoring/IoT use cases.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity. Requires learning time-series concepts (tags vs fields, measurement schemas, retention policies), InfluxQL or Flux query language (different from SQL), and understanding cardinality implications for performance. Setup involves configuring retention policies, continuous queries/tasks for downsampling, and integration with collectors (Telegraf, custom agents). Data modeling is critical - poor schema design causes severe performance issues. Team needs 2-4 weeks for proper implementation including monitoring migration, query optimization, and establishing best practices. Higher than standard SQL databases due to specialized domain knowledge.
provenance.licenseCost: InfluxDB OSS is open-source (MIT license) with no licensing costs. InfluxDB Enterprise and Cloud offer commercial features (clustering, high availability, enhanced security) but free tier and OSS version are production-capable for most use cases. No upfront license purchase required - Cloud operates on consumption-based pricing included in monthly costs, Enterprise uses annual subscription but not mandatory.

supports_nfr.reason: InfluxDB Enterprise clustering with automated failover achieves up to 99.9% availability as a ceiling.
InfluxDB as a time-series database achieves p95 latency of at least 5ms for write and query operations.
InfluxDB p99 latency is at least 20ms under typical time-series workload conditions.
InfluxDB Enterprise with automated failover achieves 5-10 minute RTO for recovery from node failures.
InfluxDB Enterprise with synchronous replication across nodes achieves near-zero RPO of approximately 1 minute.
InfluxDB supports high write throughput up to 100k writes/second (360M per hour) as a ceiling.
InfluxDB handles up to 50k read queries per second for time-series analytical queries.
InfluxDB is optimized for high write throughput up to 100k writes per second for time-stamped data ingestion.
InfluxDB Enterprise provides comprehensive audit logging for queries, writes, and configuration changes, but OSS version does not.
InfluxDB supports multi-tenancy through database-per-tenant or tag-based row-level isolation for time-series data segmentation.
InfluxDB can store any data including PII with appropriate security controls and encryption at rest and in transit.
InfluxDB supports GDPR compliance through retention policies, data deletion APIs, and access controls for time-series data.
InfluxDB can meet HIPAA requirements with encryption at rest and in transit, audit logging, and access controls when properly configured.
InfluxDB supports CCPA compliance through data retention policies, user data deletion capabilities, and audit trails.
InfluxDB supports SOX compliance through audit logging, data immutability features, and access control mechanisms for financial time-series data.
InfluxDB provides eventual consistency by default but supports read-your-writes through single-node writes and consistent query routing.
InfluxDB provides strict durability through write-ahead log (WAL) with fsync before acknowledging writes when configured.

supports_constraints.reason: InfluxDB can be self-hosted on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure as a portable time-series database.
InfluxDB is a database infrastructure component accessed via HTTP API and client libraries available for all major languages.
InfluxDB is a backend time-series database suitable for API services, IoT telemetry, batch processing, and data pipeline workloads.
InfluxDB is specifically a time-series database and requires the time_series_db feature flag to be true for activation.
InfluxDB can be used with or without external caching layers depending on application read patterns and query frequency.
InfluxDB works with both synchronous write patterns and async messaging systems like Kafka for time-series data ingestion.
InfluxDB can store time-series data for AI/ML training and inference workloads or be used independently of AI features.
InfluxDB supports multi-tenant deployments through database-per-tenant or tag-based isolation, but also works for single-tenant applications.
InfluxDB supports both real-time and batch ingestion of time-series data through continuous queries and bulk write APIs.
InfluxDB does not natively support distributed transactions but can participate in eventual consistency patterns with other systems.
InfluxDB excels at real-time streaming data ingestion for IoT and monitoring use cases but also supports batch workloads.
InfluxDB is a time-series database and does not provide vector search capabilities but can coexist with vector databases.
InfluxDB is a time-series database, not a document store, but can coexist with document databases in polyglot persistence architectures.
InfluxDB is a time-series database with tag-based indexing, not a key-value store, but can complement key-value stores in architectures.
InfluxDB is a time-series database without graph query capabilities but can coexist with graph databases for relationship data.
InfluxDB is optimized for time-series writes and queries, not traditional OLTP, but can support transactional inserts alongside OLAP queries.
InfluxDB supports analytical queries over time-series data with downsampling and aggregations, fitting OLAP patterns, but also handles operational writes.

warn_nfr.reason: InfluxDB is optimized for high-volume time-series ingestion. At <10 writes/s, a simpler general-purpose database may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: db-timeseries--influxdb: peak write QPS is {actual} writes/s (<10). InfluxDB's time-series optimizations may be overkill for this low write volume; consider a simpler database.

warn_nfr.reason: InfluxDB excels at high-frequency data ingestion. At <10 writes/s (36k/hour), operational overhead may outweigh benefits.
warn_nfr.message: db-timeseries--influxdb: peak ingestion is {actual} writes/hour (<36k, or ~10/s). Time-series database overhead may not be justified at this scale.
